headline: Tennessee's ground game and MacIntyre's efficiency overwhelm Clemson's aerial attack
body: Tennessee came to Death Valley and imposed its will on Clemson in a season-opening statement, controlling the line of scrimmage for four quarters en route to a 35-20 victory. The Volunteers' balanced attack—155 rushing yards and 444 passing yards—kept the Clemson defense honest and prevented the Tigers from ever settling into a rhythm despite James Cortez's respectable performance through the air.

Cortez threw for 303 yards and two touchdowns on 20 of 39 attempts, earning Player of the Game honors largely on the strength of his connection with Isaiah Fields, who hauled in six catches for 139 yards and a score. Yet those numbers mask a fundamental problem: Clemson's running game never materialized, with Adams Jr. managing just 16 yards on eight carries. That offensive imbalance allowed Tennessee's defense to tee off on Cortez, who was forced to beat them through the air alone.

MacIntyre's 23-for-34 efficiency and 447 yards epitomized Tennessee's poise. The Volunteers didn't need to get fancy. Bishop punched in two rushing touchdowns, Jalen Smith created constant separation downfield with ten receptions for 242 yards, and the offensive line allowed just enough time for MacIntyre to operate methodically. Clemson's lone turnover—a lost fumble—didn't sink them, but the yardage disparity of 599 to 348 told the real story of a team that couldn't match Tennessee's execution on either side of the ball.
turningPoint: Bishop's first rushing touchdown midway through the second quarter shifted momentum decisively to Tennessee, establishing confidence in the ground game and forcing Clemson to abandon any balanced attack philosophy.

unsungHero.name: Jalen Smith
unsungHero.why: Smith's 242 receiving yards and consistent possession chains enabled Tennessee's offense to control time of possession and rhythm, yet MacIntyre's efficient passing gets the spotlight while Smith's volume and reliability go overlooked.
